title: Language breadth begins: Python lands via the LanguageSpec registry (charter exception, deliberately)
context: The charter (dec_0db7028076) rejected breadth-FIRST: shallow multi-language support before the core loop proved out. The core loop has now shipped end to end (v1.8.0: auto-trust, self-repair, inline escalations, certified VS Code client, released to npm + Open VSX). Contributor MrSampson's PR #9 adds Python through a LanguageSpec registry that collapses the three duplicated JS/TS extension gates into one entry point.
decision: Accept Python support via PR #9's registry design. The charter's rejection was of breadth-first, not breadth-ever — with the core proven, each new language is one registry entry plus one tree-sitter grammar dependency, which is breadth done cheaply AFTER the core. Constraints that keep it honest: (1) every added grammar must ship prebuilds for win32/linux/darwin and ride the per-process temp-copy loader (the EBUSY class stays dead); (2) Constitution mutation controls and behavior evaluators remain TS-scoped until separately proven — indexing breadth is not evaluator breadth; (3) import resolution keeps the don't-guess-on-ambiguity rule.
consequences: Python repos get symbols, call edges, imports, blast radius, and backfill.
The registry is the template: future languages are a PR-sized addition, not a rewrite.
One more native dependency enters the temp-copy loader's care.
alternatives_rejected: Hold Python until after the G4 pilot (rejected: the registry work is done, external momentum is real, and indexing breadth doesn't touch the governance evidence chain the pilot gates)

Accept indexing AND extend Constitution evaluators to Python in one step (rejected: evaluator semantics need their own proofs; conflating them is exactly the shallow-breadth failure the charter feared)
